description: The provided image is an irrelevant generic user interface button labeled "Check for updates", consisting of a red bookmark-shaped graphic inside a partial circular ring with upper blue and lower yellow segments, on a light gray gradient background. This image has no correlation to the content of the attached study focusing on grain size optimization for enhanced corrosion resistance of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. No material science-related information including microstructure characterization, performance test data, synthesis processes, or experimental results can be extracted from this mismatched image.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) after different solution treatment processes. Four tested samples include S0 (hot-rolled and water-quenched without further solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃ for 2 h followed by water quenching), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃ for 2 h followed by water quenching), and S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃ for 2 h followed by water quenching). The x-axis is 2θ ranging from 30 deg to 90 deg, and the y-axis refers to diffraction intensity in arbitrary units (a.u.). All samples show characteristic diffraction peaks assigned to single-phase face-centered cubic (FCC) austenite, corresponding to (111), (200), (220) and (311) crystallographic planes. As solution treatment temperature rises, the intensity of (111) peak increases remarkably while the intensity of (220) peak decreases accordingly, which proves that solution treatment adjusts the alloy texture while retaining the austenitic matrix. This result confirms all prepared samples are single FCC phase, providing a solid phase composition basis for subsequent study on the effect of grain size optimization on corrosion resistance of LDSs.

This set of micrographs presents the microstructure of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) after different processing routes, with a uniform 30 μm scale bar for each subfigure. Subfigure (a) corresponds to sample S0 (hot rolled and water quenched without solution treatment), showing non-uniform grains containing deformed structures, nanoscale κ-carbides and high dislocation density. Subfigure (b) displays sample S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃ for 2 h then water quenched), featuring fine equiaxed face-centered cubic austenite grains with average size of 8.7 μm and high density of annealing twin boundaries. Subfigure (c) shows sample S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃ for 2 h) with medium-sized equiaxed austenite grains. Subfigure (d) presents sample S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃ for 2h), with coarse equiaxed austenite grains of 32 μm average size and low twin boundary density. The micrographs verify that LDS grain size increases with rising solution temperature, supporting the core conclusion that grain size optimization via solution treatment regulates oxide film formation and corrosion resistance of LDSs.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization results of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) for four samples: S0 (no solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 °C for 2 h), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 °C for 2 h), S1150 (solution treated at 1150 °C for 2 h). Each sample corresponds to three maps: inverse pole figure (IPF) maps (a/b/c/d) showing grain orientation, confirming no preferred orientation for all solution-treated samples, with average grain size increasing from 8.7 μm to 32 μm as solution temperature rises while S0 has non-uniform grain size distribution; kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps (a1/b1/c1/d1) reflecting residual stress, where S0 shows the highest residual stress with average KAM of 1.62, which reduces to ~0.4 after solution treatment; grain boundary (GB) distribution maps (a2/b2/c2/d2) marking 2-10° low-angle GBs in blue and >10° high-angle GBs in red, showing S0 contains abundant low-angle GBs, while recrystallization after solution treatment produces high-density annealing twin boundaries and high-angle GBs, with S950 having the highest GB density that decreases with increasing solution temperature. These results reveal microstructural evolution induced by solution treatment, supporting the correlation between grain structure and corrosion resistance of LDSs.

This figure presents grain size distribution histograms (subplots a-d) and grain boundary property plus kernel average misorientation (KAM) statistical data (subplot e) for four Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel (LDS) samples: as-hot-rolled S0, S950 (950℃ solution treated), S1050 (1050℃ solution treated), and S1150 (1150℃ solution treated). Average grain size increases from 7.6 μm (S0) to 8.7 μm (S950), 21.1 μm (S1050), and 32.0 μm (S1150), rising with increasing solution treatment temperature. Subplot e shows annealing twin boundary (ATB) and high-angle grain boundary (HAGB) densities (left y-axis, μm/μm²), and average KAM (right y-axis, representing residual stress): S0 has the highest KAM (~1.62), while S950 has the highest ATB (0.146) and HAGB (0.114) densities, which decrease with higher solution temperature, and KAM drops to ~0.4 for all solution-treated samples. The data demonstrates that solution treatment reduces residual stress and increases grain size, with S950 achieving the highest grain boundary density to facilitate Al-rich oxide film formation for improved corrosion resistance.

This set of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images characterizes the microstructures of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs). Subfigure (a) shows bright-field TEM morphology of the as-hot-rolled S0 sample, where yellow arrows mark dispersed nanoscale κ-carbides with size ranging from 5 nm to 10 nm. Subfigure (a1) presents the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the marked κ-carbide and corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns, confirming both the κ-carbide and austenitic matrix have [110] zone axis orientation. Subfigure (a2) shows the dislocation morphology of S0 sample with corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern verifying the face-centered cubic (FCC) austenite matrix, which contains high density of irregularly distributed dislocations. Subfigure (b) displays the twin morphology of solution heat-treated LDS, with SAED pattern confirming the orientation relationship between austenite matrix and annealing twin, where dislocation density decreases after heat treatment and annealing twin boundaries effectively hinder dislocation movement. These TEM results reveal the microstructural evolution of LDSs before and after solution treatment, supporting subsequent correlation analysis between microstructure and corrosion resistance.

This figure presents electrochemical corrosion performance of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with different grain sizes adjusted via solution treatment temperature in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Subplot (a) shows potentiodynamic polarization curves for four samples: S0 (no solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃ for 2 h), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃ for 2 h), S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃ for 2 h), with y-axis as potential versus SCE and x-axis as current density. Subplot (b) displays the corresponding breakdown current density (i_bre, unit: μA/cm²) derived from the polarization curves. The measured i_bre values are 9.92 μA/cm² for S0, 2.72 μA/cm² for S950, 6.55 μA/cm² for S1050, and 8.84 μA/cm² for S1150. As lower i_bre indicates better corrosion resistance, the fine-grained S950 sample exhibits the highest corrosion resistance, while the untreated S0 shows the poorest corrosion resistance. This supports the core conclusion that grain refinement promotes the formation of protective Al-rich oxide films, thus enhancing the corrosion resistance of LDSs.

This group of SEM micrographs presents the surface corrosion morphology of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) after potentiodynamic polarization to 5 mA/cm² for four differently processed samples: (a) S0, the as-hot-rolled sample without further solution treatment, showing severe corrosion damage with abundant deep pitting pits due to high surface residual stress and heterogeneous grain boundary distribution; (b) S950, solution treated at 950 ℃ for 2 h followed by water quenching, exhibiting only a few small pitting pits, indicating the optimal corrosion resistance among the four samples; (c) S1050, solution treated at 1050 ℃ for 2 h, showing increased number and size of pitting pits; (d) S1150, solution treated at 1150 ℃ for 2 h, presenting further enlarged pitting pits and visible pitting corrosion expansion, representing degraded corrosion resistance. This figure supports the core conclusion that grain refinement via lower solution treatment temperature significantly improves the corrosion resistance of LDSs, as fine grains promote the formation of uniform, stable Al-rich protective oxide film to suppress pitting initiation and development.

This figure presents electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) tested in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, containing three subplots: (a) Nyquist plot with a fitted equivalent circuit (R_s: solution resistance, Q_1/R_1: constant phase element/ion diffusion resistance at the solution/oxide film interface, Q_2/R_2: constant phase element/oxide film resistance); (b) Bode magnitude plot; (c) Bode phase plot. Four samples are tested: S0 (without post-rolling solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃ for 2 h), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃ for 2 h), S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃ for 2 h). Key trends: The arc radius in Nyquist plots follows the order S950 > S1050 > S1150 > S0, indicating S950 has the highest polarization resistance; Bode plots show the highest low-frequency impedance modulus for S950, and phase angle peaks between 65° and 75° at intermediate frequencies, confirming two time constants for all samples. These results demonstrate that finer-grained samples prepared at lower solution treatment temperature exhibit better corrosion resistance, supporting the conclusion that grain refinement promotes the formation of protective Al-rich oxide films.

This bar chart displays the corrosion rate (unit: mm/y) of four Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel (LDS) samples after 30 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The tested samples include: S0 (as-hot-rolled without subsequent solution treatment, with non-uniform grain size, κ-carbide precipitates and high dislocation density), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃ for 2 h then water quenched, fine average grain size 8.7 μm), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃ for 2 h then water quenched, medium grain size), and S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃ for 2 h then water quenched, coarse average grain size 32 μm). Measured corrosion rates are 0.326 mm/y for S0, 0.160 mm/y for S950, 0.248 mm/y for S1050, and 0.281 mm/y for S1150. The trend shows that grain refinement via lower-temperature solution treatment drastically reduces corrosion rate, with the finest-grained S950 sample exhibiting the lowest corrosion rate (43% lower than coarse-grained S1150). This figure supports the core conclusion that optimized grain size promotes formation of protective Al-rich oxide films, significantly improving corrosion resistance of LDSs in chloride-containing environments.

This figure presents potentiostatic polarization curves of four Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel (LDS) samples tested in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, with x-axis as polarization time (range 0 to 8000 s) and y-axis as current density (unit: ×10⁻⁴ A/cm²), plus an inset that magnifies the stable current region at 6400 s to 7400 s. The four samples are S0 (no post-hot-rolling solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃ for 2 h), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃ for 2 h), and S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃ for 2 h). All samples exhibit a rapid drop in current density within the first 60 s, corresponding to initial oxide film formation to inhibit anodic dissolution, followed by a current rise then fall between 600 s and 1400 s, corresponding to oxide film rupture and self-healing. The stable current densities follow the order S0 (8.39 μA/cm², highest) > S1150 (6.04 μA/cm²) > S1050 (4.52 μA/cm²) > S950 (2.45 μA/cm², lowest), indicating the fine-grained S950 sample forms the most protective oxide film. The results support the core conclusion that grain size optimization improves LDS corrosion resistance by promoting formation of stable, protective Al-rich oxide films.

This figure presents electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) after potentiostatic polarization in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, containing three subplots: (a) Nyquist plot with a corresponding equivalent circuit for data fitting, (b) Bode magnitude plot, and (c) Bode phase plot. Four samples are tested: S0 (hot rolled and quenched without post solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃ for 2 h), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃ for 2 h), S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃ for 2 h). The Nyquist plot shows that S950 has the largest capacitive arc radius, while S0 has the smallest, and the radius decreases with increasing grain size (rising solution treatment temperature). The Bode magnitude plot reveals S950 maintains the highest low-frequency impedance modulus, which decreases as grain size grows. The Bode phase plot indicates S950 retains a higher phase angle over a wider frequency range. These results confirm that the fine-grained S950 sample forms a denser, more stable Al-rich oxide film with superior corrosion protection performance, demonstrating grain size optimization effectively enhances the corrosion resistance of LDSs.

This figure consists of two subplots characterizing the semiconductor properties and defect densities of oxide films formed on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Subplot (a) shows Mott-Schottky curves (C⁻² plotted against potential E vs. SCE) for four samples: S0 (no post-hot-rolling solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃), S1050 (1050 ℃ solution treated), S1150 (1150 ℃ solution treated). All samples exhibit duplex semiconductor behavior: Region 1 (-1.13 to -0.93 V<sub>SCE</sub>) shows positive slopes (n-type semiconductor, dominated by oxygen vacancies and cation interstitials), while Region 2 (-0.93 to -0.67 V<sub>SCE</sub>) shows negative slopes (p-type semiconductor, dominated by cation vacancies). Subplot (b) is a bar chart of calculated donor (N<sub>D</sub>, purple) and acceptor (N<sub>A</sub>, cyan) carrier concentrations. S0 has the highest N<sub>D</sub> (0.70×10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>) and N<sub>A</sub> (2.25×10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>), while S950 has the lowest N<sub>D</sub> (0.26×10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>) and N<sub>A</sub> (0.68×10<sup>21</sup> cm<sup>-3</sup>); carrier concentrations increase as solution treatment temperature rises from 950 ℃ to 1150 ℃. Lower N<sub>D</sub> and N<sub>A</sub> correspond to fewer oxide film defects and reduced pathways for chloride ion penetration, confirming that appropriately solution-treated fine-grained LDSs form more protective oxide films, supporting the conclusion that grain size optimization enhances LDS corrosion resistance.

This group of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra characterizes the elemental chemical valence states of surface oxide films formed on four Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel (LDS) samples after 7200 s potentiostatic polarization in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution: S0 (no post-rolling solution treatment), S950 (950 ℃ solution treated), S1050 (1050 ℃ solution treated), S1150 (1150 ℃ solution treated). Subfigures (a-d) show deconvoluted Fe 2p3/2 spectra (Fe²+_OX, Fe³+_OX, Fe³+_hy, plus an extra Fe²+_sat peak for S950); (a1-d1) show Mn 2p3/2 spectra (Mn²+, Mn⁴+, Mn²+_sat, with S950 only containing Mn²+); (a2-d2) show Al 2p spectra with a single Al³+ peak assigned to Al oxides/hydroxides; (a3-d3) show O 1s spectra deconvoluted to O²- (oxide), OH⁻ (hydroxide), and H2O (crystalline water). The results confirm the chemical composition of Al-rich oxide films on fine-grained samples, supporting the conclusion that grain refinement promotes formation of stable protective oxide films to improve corrosion resistance.

This 3D stacked bar chart displays the cation fraction (unit: at.%) of different valence states of metallic elements in the oxide films formed on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with varied grain sizes, prepared by different heat treatments. The tested samples include S0 (hot-rolled, no post solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 °C, finest grain size ~8.7 μm), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 °C), and S1150 (solution treated at 1150 °C, coarsest grain size ~32 μm). Quantified valence states cover Al³⁺, Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺_OX (oxide phase Fe³⁺), Fe³⁺_hy (hydroxide phase Fe³⁺), Mn⁴⁺, and Mn²⁺. Key observations show Al³⁺ is the dominant cation in all oxide films; Al content gradually decreases as grain size increases (rising solution treatment temperature), while Fe and Mn contents correspondingly increase. The S950 sample exhibits the highest Al content (90.17 at.%) in its oxide film. This figure supports the conclusion that grain refinement promotes preferential Al dissolution and Al-rich oxide film formation, improving LDS corrosion resistance.

This figure presents Auger electron spectroscopy (AES) depth profiling results of oxide films on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with four different preparation states: S0 (no post-hot-rolling solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃), and S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃). Subplot (a) displays atomic concentration profiles of O, Fe, Al, Mn against sputtering time for each sample separately, while subplot (b) compares concentration trends of each individual element across all four samples. Oxide film thickness, determined as the depth where O concentration reduces to half of its maximum value, is 16.2±0.8 nm for S0, 25.2±1.2 nm for S950, 21.6±1 nm for S1050, and 18±0.9 nm for S1150. The results indicate that oxide film thickness and Al content decrease with increasing grain size (higher solution treatment temperature), and the fine-grained S950 forms the thickest, most uniform Al-rich oxide film, validating that grain refinement improves LDS corrosion resistance via promoting protective oxide film formation.

This set of figures presents three calculated Pourbaix (E-pH) diagrams for the Fe-Mn-Al-H₂O system at 25 °C, 1 atm, with Fe, Mn, Al ion concentrations all set to 1×10⁻¹⁰ M, corresponding to the corrosion environment of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs). Subplot (a) maps stability regions of Al-containing phases, (b) maps Fe-containing phases, (c) maps Mn-containing phases, with x-axis as pH ranging from -2 to 14 and y-axis as potential vs. SHE (V) ranging from -2.0 to 2.0. The pink circle in each subplot marks the experimental corrosion test conditions of the study, while dashed lines indicate stability boundaries of O₂ and H₂ at 1 atm. Under the experimental conditions, Al locates in the Al₂O₃(H₂O) stable zone, confirming spontaneous formation of dense, stable Al-rich oxide film. Fe is primarily stable as Fe²⁺, and Mn stays in the Mn²⁺ stable zone with adjacent MnO₂ region, indicating thermodynamic feasibility of Mn oxidation at appropriate potentials. These results support the mechanism that Al-driven protective oxide film formation enhances LDS corrosion resistance.

This figure presents initial-stage corrosion dissolution behavior and surface potential distribution of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with varying grain sizes. Subfigures (a, a₁) are low- and high-magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of fine-grained sample S950 (solution treated at 950 °C, average grain size 8.7 μm), explicitly marking grain boundary dissolution and intragranular dissolution features. Subfigures (b, b₁) are corresponding SEM images of coarse-grained sample S1150 (solution treated at 1150 °C, average grain size 32 μm), also exhibiting both corrosion dissolution modes. Subfigures (c) and (d) are Scanning Kelvin Probe Force Microscopy (SKPFM) surface potential maps (potential range: -30 mV to 30 mV) of annealing twin boundaries (ATBs) and high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs), respectively, with Line A and Line B marked for potential profiling. Subfigure (e) displays the potential distribution along the two lines: ATBs show ~10–15 mV higher potential than the austenite matrix, while HAGBs show ~4–19 mV lower potential than the matrix. These results confirm that HAGBs act as preferential anodic dissolution sites at the initial corrosion stage, supporting the core conclusion that high grain boundary density in fine-grained LDS provides abundant nucleation sites for uniform, stable Al-rich oxide film formation, thus enhancing corrosion resistance.

This figure plots the polarization resistance (Rₚ, unit: Ω·cm²) of surface oxide films on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) as a function of immersion time (0 to 168 h) in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, with Rₚ values derived from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) fittings. Four tested samples include S0 (no post-rolling solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃, fine-grained with average grain size 8.7 μm), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃, medium-grained), and S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃, coarse-grained with average grain size 32 μm). The fine-grained S950 sample exhibits the highest Rₚ across the entire immersion period, reaching ~1.15×10⁵ Ω·cm² at 168 h, which is significantly higher than the ~5×10⁴ Ω·cm² of coarse-grained S1150. Rₚ of S950 rises rapidly in the initial 2-8 h of immersion and stabilizes after approximately 24 h, indicating fast formation of a dense, stable protective oxide film. Coarser-grained samples show lower initial Rₚ and slower Rₚ growth, corresponding to delayed oxide film formation and inferior protective performance. This figure supports the core conclusion that grain refinement of LDSs improves corrosion resistance by promoting rapid formation of protective Al-rich oxide films.

This figure presents cyclic voltammetry curves of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) tested in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, with two subplots for the first cycle (a) and second cycle (b). Three samples are compared: S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃, finest grain size), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃, medium grain size), and S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃, coarsest grain size). In the first cycle, an Al oxidation peak appears at ~-0.81 V_SCE, with S950 showing the highest peak current density, indicating higher Al oxidation activity that promotes rapid Al-rich oxide film formation; no Fe/Mn oxidation peak is observed as the formed Al-rich film suppresses Fe/Mn oxidation. In the second cycle, an additional Fe/Mn oxidation peak emerges at ~-0.56 V_SCE, where S950 exhibits the lowest peak intensity, confirming its dense protective Al-rich oxide film inhibits further Fe/Mn oxidation, while S1050 and S1150 show higher peak intensities corresponding to incomplete, less protective surface films. These results support the core conclusion that grain refinement facilitates formation of dense Al-rich oxide film to enhance corrosion resistance of LDSs.

This set of schematic diagrams illustrates the oxide film formation and corrosion mechanisms of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with different grain sizes. Subfigures (a), (a1), (a2), (a3) correspond to the small grain size sample (average grain size 8.7 μm, S950 sample), while (b), (b1), (b2), (b3) correspond to the large grain size sample (average grain size 32 μm, S1150 sample). The fine-grained sample has high density of high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) and annealing twin boundaries (ATBs), which provide abundant nucleation sites for oxide film growth. After preferential dissolution of active grain boundary regions, a uniform, dense Al-rich oxide film forms on the surface, effectively resisting chloride ion erosion and achieving high corrosion resistance. The coarse-grained sample has low grain boundary density, leading to sparse nucleation sites, so the formed oxide film has high defect density, with visible cracks and corrosion pits, showing poor corrosion resistance. The figure supports the conclusion that grain refinement promotes the formation of stable Al-rich oxide film, thus improving the corrosion resistance of LDSs.
